news_summary: Humans continue to excel in complex mathematical reasoning, outperforming AI on challenging tests.
news_summary_fr: Les humains continuent d'exceller dans le raisonnement mathématique complexe, surpassant l'IA lors de tests difficiles.
news_summary_es: Los seres humanos siguen destacando en el razonamiento matemático complejo, superando a la IA en pruebas exigentes.

articles.title: Humans outperform AI at this highly rigorous mathematics test
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 12 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01888-9A new benchmark pitting AI against previously unseen maths problems shows systems still fall short of top human expertise.
articles.content:
The top-performing artificial-intelligence model scored 6 out of 10 in the First Proof set of mathematical challenges.Credit: vitacopS/Getty

Artificial intelligence has undergone its most scrupulous maths test yet. The results are in, and the AI models that took part didn’t live up to the problem-solving skills of top mathematicians.

The test — part of a project called First Proof, which aims to evaluate the ability of AI to solve complex questions in mathematics — posed ten research-level maths problems to four AI systems. A jury of anonymous human specialists in the relevant mathematical fields then assessed the models’ answers. This test was the first of its kind to satisfy three key conditions simultaneously: first, it consisted of research-level maths questions; second, it involved problems that did not appear in the training data; and third, it was formally graded by mathematicians. The results were unveiled on the First Proof website on 10 June.

These findings follow recent AI breakthroughs in solving maths problems. Last month, for example, a chatbot made by the technology firm OpenAI, in San Francisco, California, solved an 80-year-old maths challenge set by the late mathematician Paul Erdős. The First Proof team says that future iterations of the test could help researchers to judge how useful AI models could be for mathematicians; for example, in solving problems autonomously, checking proofs or acting as research assistants.

Prove this

One important innovation of the First Proof test was that the questions had not previously been mentioned anywhere in the published literature or on the Internet — cutting the risk that the models could simply be regurgitating information they had learnt during their training. Instead, ten researchers from a broad range of mathematical specialities each provided a question that they had solved in the course of their own research but had not yet published.

First Proof ran a trial test in February with a different batch of novel problems. In that round, anyone could try their own favourite AI systems on the problems, and many groups did — but the results were not officially verified by the First Proof team. There was also no way to independently check that the AIs had not received any help from humans.

AI cracks 80-year-old mathematics challenge — researchers are astonished

This time, First Proof ran the test itself: the team asked the models to solve problems in an entirely autonomous way, and had a group of 30 mathematicians vet the answers. “The organizers have clearly thought through the second batch more carefully to make it more controlled and systematic,” says mathematician Jeremy Avigad, who heads the Institute for Computer-Aided Reasoning in Mathematics at Carnegie Mellon University in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

Another rule was that the participating models had to be publicly available. This meant that Google’s Aletheia — a system designed specifically for solving maths problems — and the full, unreleased version of Claude Mythos, a model made by Anthropic in San Francisco, California, could not be used. OpenAI was the only big company that participated, with its model ChatGPT 5.5 Pro.

The other systems were provided by three academic groups, from the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA); Princeton University in New Jersey; and the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology (ETH) in Zurich. All three built ‘harnesses’ on top of existing chatbots, such as ChatGPT, Google’s Gemini and the publicly available version of Anthropic’s Claude. (A harness is an automated system that asks a chatbot a question and has the answer checked by another chatbot, often with repeated back-and-forth.)

Maths results

The ETH team’s model performed the best, solving six out of ten problems with a system in which ChatGPT’s answers were vetted or improved on by an ‘advisory council’ made up of all three major chatbots. The UCLA team, which built a harness on top of ChatGPT, was the second best, followed by the OpenAI team (ChatGPT with no harness) and Princeton (a harness using mainly Gemini 3.1 Pro as its backend).
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a rigorous, novel benchmark evaluating AI's ability to solve complex, previously unseen research-level mathematics problems, showing that current AI still falls short of top human mathematicians. This highlights meaningful progress and sets a clear path for future AI development with potential broad impact on mathematical research and problem-solving.
articles.personality_title: New math test shows top humans still beat AI in tough problems
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A project called First Proof created a new kind of math test to see how well artificial intelligence (AI) can solve very hard math problems that no one has published before. The test was carefully checked by expert mathematicians.

**What happened** – In June 2026, four AI systems tried to solve ten research-level math problems. These problems were new, meaning the AIs had not seen them before. A group of 30 mathematicians checked the AI answers. The best AI system solved 6 out of 10 problems, but no AI matched the skill of top human mathematicians.

**Impact** – This test is special because it used new math problems that were never online or in books, so the AIs could not just copy answers. It showed that even advanced AI models still struggle with difficult math questions compared to human experts. The test helps researchers understand where AI needs to improve to be more useful in math research.

**What's next step** – The First Proof team plans to keep improving the test and seeing how AI can help mathematicians in the future, for example, by checking math proofs or assisting with research. They hope AI will get better at solving new problems on its own.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new, carefully designed math test proves that current AI can handle some tough problems but still falls short of expert human mathematicians.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau test de mathématiques montre que les meilleurs humains surpassent encore l’IA sur des problèmes difficiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Un projet appelé First Proof a créé un nouveau test de mathématiques pour évaluer la capacité de l’intelligence artificielle (IA) à résoudre des problèmes très complexes, jamais publiés auparavant. Ce test a été vérifié par des mathématiciens experts.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En juin 2026, quatre systèmes d’IA ont tenté de résoudre dix problèmes mathématiques de niveau recherche. Ces problèmes étaient nouveaux, donc inconnus des IA. Un groupe de 30 mathématiciens a évalué les réponses des IA. Le meilleur système a résolu 6 problèmes sur 10, mais aucune IA n’a égalé les meilleurs mathématiciens humains.

**Impact** – Ce test est unique car il utilise des problèmes inédits, empêchant les IA de simplement copier des réponses. Il montre que même les IA avancées ont encore des difficultés avec des questions mathématiques complexes comparées aux experts humains. Ce test aide les chercheurs à comprendre où l’IA doit progresser pour être plus utile en recherche mathématique.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe First Proof prévoit d’améliorer le test et d’étudier comment l’IA peut aider les mathématiciens, par exemple en vérifiant des preuves ou en assistant la recherche. Ils espèrent que l’IA deviendra plus autonome pour résoudre de nouveaux problèmes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau test rigoureux montre que l’IA actuelle peut résoudre certains problèmes difficiles, mais reste en retrait par rapport aux meilleurs mathématiciens humains.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva prueba matemática muestra que los mejores humanos aún superan a la IA en problemas difíciles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un proyecto llamado First Proof creó una nueva prueba de matemáticas para evaluar qué tan bien la inteligencia artificial (IA) puede resolver problemas muy complejos que nunca se habían publicado antes. La prueba fue revisada por matemáticos expertos.

**Qué pasó** – En junio de 2026, cuatro sistemas de IA intentaron resolver diez problemas matemáticos de nivel de investigación. Estos problemas eran nuevos, es decir, las IA no los habían visto antes. Un grupo de 30 matemáticos revisó las respuestas de la IA. El mejor sistema de IA resolvió 6 de 10 problemas, pero ninguna IA alcanzó la habilidad de los mejores matemáticos humanos.

**Impacto** – Esta prueba es especial porque usó problemas nuevos, evitando que las IA solo copiaran respuestas. Demostró que incluso los modelos avanzados de IA todavía tienen dificultades con preguntas matemáticas difíciles en comparación con expertos humanos. La prueba ayuda a los investigadores a entender dónde la IA necesita mejorar para ser más útil en la investigación matemática.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de First Proof planea seguir mejorando la prueba y ver cómo la IA puede ayudar a los matemáticos en el futuro, por ejemplo, verificando pruebas matemáticas o asistiendo en la investigación. Esperan que la IA mejore para resolver problemas nuevos por sí sola.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva prueba cuidadosamente diseñada muestra que la IA actual puede manejar algunos problemas difíciles, pero todavía queda detrás de los matemáticos humanos expertos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a majestic open book glowing softly with complex mathematical symbols and elegant geometric shapes rising like constellations above it, surrounded by four distinct, stylized abstract orbs representing AI models, with one radiant orb subtly smaller and a golden hue symbolizing human intellect gently illuminating the scene.
